nameMeanings.name: Witchingham
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016

variants.name: Kitchenham
meanings: English: locative name from Kitchingham (in Etchingham, Sussex) or Kitchenham (in Peasmarsh, Sussex).

nameMeanings.name: Amy
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Anglicized form of Old French Amee ‘beloved’. This originated in part as a vernacular nickname, in part as a form of Latin Amata. The latter is ostensibly the feminine form of the past participle of amare ‘to love’, but in fact it may have had a different, pre-Roman, origin; it was borne in classical mythology by the wife of King Latinus , whose daughter Lavinia married Aeneas and (according to the story in the Aeneid) became the mother of the Roman people.

variants.name: Aimie
meanings: Anglicized form of Old French Amee ‘beloved’. This originated in part as a vernacular nickname, in part as a form of Latin Amata. The latter is ostensibly the feminine form of the past participle of amare ‘to love’, but in fact it may have had a different, pre-Roman, origin; it was borne in classical mythology by the wife of King Latinus , whose daughter Lavinia married Aeneas and (according to the story in the Aeneid) became the mother of the Roman people.

variants.name: Aimée
meanings: originally a vernacular nickname meaning ‘beloved’, from the past participle of French aimer ‘to love’ (Latin amare; compare Amy ). It has been in use in French since the Middle Ages, although it has never been very common. It is now also used, with or without the accent, as a given name in the English-speaking world.

variants.name: Amie
meanings: Variant spelling of   Amy  , corresponding in form to the French vocabulary word meaning ‘(female) friend’.

variants.name: Ami
meanings: Anglicized form of Old French Amee ‘beloved’. This originated in part as a vernacular nickname, in part as a form of Latin Amata. The latter is ostensibly the feminine form of the past participle of amare ‘to love’, but in fact it may have had a different, pre-Roman, origin; it was borne in classical mythology by the wife of King Latinus , whose daughter Lavinia married Aeneas and (according to the story in the Aeneid) became the mother of the Roman people.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.

en.lifeSummary: Amy Witchingham was born in Coningsby, Lincolnshire, England as the daughter of Edmund Wychingham and Alice Fastolfe. She married Robert Steele on 25 February 1440, in Greenock West, Renfrewshire, Scotland. She died in 1496, in her hometown.
